turns.content: I have a working Rust program with unsafe code, and I want to catch use-after-free or out-of-bounds bugs. Is there a better way than just running it under valgrind?

Using AddressSanitizer (ASan) is definitely a more integrated way! Instead of relying on external tools like Valgrind, ASan integrates directly into the Rust compiler. It’s fast and gives you detailed runtime error messages right inside your IDE.
That sounds good. But how do I set it up?
You can enable ASan via the RUSTFLAGS environment variable with `-Z sanitizer=address`. This tells the compiler to instrument your code for memory errors at compile time.
Oh, so I just set that flag before compiling? And it’ll catch unsafe bugs?
Exactly! It’s like adding a safety net to your runtime. Just remember, ASan is only available on nightly Rust for now since it uses some experimental features.
What about integrating it into my CI pipeline?

Good idea! You can add a step where you compile with `RUSTFLAGS='-Z sanitizer=address'` and run your tests. This ensures that your unsafe code is always tested under ASan in production-like environments.
Aha, so I set the flag during compilation, then run my tests with ASan enabled?
Yes! And if you get any sanitizer errors, they’ll give you line numbers and detailed explanations. It’s like having a debugger inside your code.

I'm planning to port a legacy C project into Rust and I want to ensure my unsafe blocks are actually safe before merging. My current plan is to compile with RUSTFLAGS=-Zsanitizer=address in my CI pipeline, but I noticed some documentation says this flag only works on nightly builds. Since we're moving towards stable, do you think I can still enable ASan effectively?

You are correct that ASan is a nightly-only feature and it does not exist in the current stable toolchain yet, so your plan to use RUSTFLAGS=-Zsanitizer=address on stable will fail. The feature flag -Zsanitizer=address was introduced as an experimental capability specifically for nightly Rust because the underlying infrastructure requires changes to the standard library that are not yet backported. Since you want to catch memory safety issues like use-after-free and out-of-bounds access in your unsafe code, you must wait until stable is updated or run your compiler on a nightly build temporarily. On nightly, you can enable ASan by setting RUSTFLAGS=-Zsanitizer=address which will instrument your code to track heap allocations and detect when memory is accessed after it has been freed.

Okay, so I have to switch my CI job to use nightly Rust just for the safety checks. But I'm worried this might break other things in our build pipeline since we are used to stable tooling. Is there a way to isolate the unsafe modules or specific crates that need checking without rebuilding everything on nightly?

You cannot easily isolate specific crates or modules to run only on nightly because the sanitizer instrumentation is applied at the compiler level for the entire crate, not per-file or per-module. If you try to build a mixed environment where one part uses stable and another uses nightly with RUSTFLAGS=-Zsanitizer=address, the tooling will likely fail due to incompatible object files or missing symbols in the standard library. The most practical approach is to run your entire project on nightly for the CI safety checks, accepting that you might need to disable some features temporarily if they conflict, then once ASan reports zero errors, you can switch back to stable for production builds.

That sounds like a lot of work just for a build step. I don't want to maintain two separate pipelines. Is there an alternative way to detect these memory safety bugs without relying on the nightly toolchain flag? Maybe using something like Miri or a different sanitizer?

Miri is actually a better fit for your needs because it runs the Rust compiler in a virtual machine and can verify that your unsafe code does not violate memory safety rules, but unlike ASan, Miri is available on stable Rust. You can enable it by setting RUSTFLAGS=--miri in your build commands without needing nightly. However, remember that Miri checks for logical errors and data races more thoroughly than simple address sanitization, so while it catches many use-after-free bugs, it does not have the same depth of instrumentation as ASan would eventually provide once stable. For now, using RUSTFLAGS=--miri on stable is your best option to catch these issues without forcing a nightly dependency.

Okay, I will try running Miri in my CI pipeline. But wait, if I use Miri and it reports an error, does that mean the code is definitely unsafe? Or can false positives happen too since we are just catching bugs, not fixing them automatically?

Miri will report errors whenever your code violates its safety rules, but you must verify each reported issue manually because false positives do occur if your logic assumes certain memory states that Miri does not track. For example, if you use raw pointers in a way that bypasses some checks or if your data structures rely on specific layout assumptions that are not enforced by the sanitizer, Miri might flag something incorrectly. You should review every error message carefully to distinguish between actual bugs and false positives before deciding whether to fix them or ignore them.

I understand. So my workflow will be: run the project with RUSTFLAGS=-Zsanitizer=address on nightly first to catch basic memory errors, then run Miri on stable to verify logic? Or should I just use Miri exclusively since it is more robust than ASan?

You should not mix these two approaches because they target different aspects of safety and have incompatible tooling requirements. Since ASan requires nightly and you want stability, skip the ASan step entirely for now and rely on Miri with RUSTFLAGS=--miri as your primary mechanism to catch memory safety issues in unsafe code. Once your project passes all Miri checks consistently across multiple runs, you can be confident that your unsafe blocks are safe enough for production without needing nightly-specific flags.

One last question: when I finally get ASan into stable Rust, will the syntax change? Will RUSTFLAGS=-Zsanitizer=address become a permanent flag like --test or something similar so we don't have to remember it manually every time?

You cannot predict exactly how the feature will be stabilized because the Rust team decides when and how features are promoted based on community feedback and testing. Even if ASan becomes stable, it might not become a permanent RUSTFLAGS option immediately; it could be exposed through Cargo configuration or new compiler flags that require specific versions of the toolchain. Until then, your best bet is to stick with Miri for now while waiting for the Rust team to finalize their plans for bringing address sanitization into stable releases.

Thanks for clarifying the distinction between ASan and Miri and why I should avoid mixing them. I will update our CI pipeline to use RUSTFLAGS=--miri instead of the nightly flag until we have more data on stability.

That is a solid plan to move forward safely without breaking your build infrastructure prematurely. Remember that while Miri is powerful, it does not replace the need for careful manual review of unsafe blocks, so keep documenting your assumptions and test cases alongside your automated checks.
